coredata.dc:title: Conformational fixation induced fluorescence turn-on of oxytetracycline coordinated on aluminum-based metal-organic frameworks for ultrasensitive sensing application
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Pengchen
Yu, Long
Ai, Yuejie
Zhang, Songlin
Ge, Hongwei
Bu, Yiming
Huang, Dejian
Wang, Xiangke
Wang, Suhua
coredata.dc:description: Since the abuse of oxytetracycline (OTC) has brought serious environmental problems, it is of great practical significance to develop sensitive OTC fluorescent probes to quantify environmental OTC residues. Herein, the aluminum-based metal-organic framework MIL-53(Al) with microflower morphology and plentiful surface open aluminum sites (denoted as FMIL-53(Al)) was tailored via an acetic acid-assisted solvothermal method and subsequently exploited for ultrasensitive detection of OTC. Compared with conventional hydrothermally synthesized MIL-53(Al), FMIL-53(Al) possesses a larger specific surface with plentiful open aluminum sites, which can selectively coordinate with OTC to fix the molecular conformation. The coordinated OTC molecules undergo a transition from twisted-conformation to extended-conformation, accompanied by an increase in fluorescence quantum yield. As a fluorescent probe, the detection limit of FMIL-53(Al) for OTC is estimated as low as 0.94 nM. Finally, the FMIL-53(Al)-based probe can be applied for the detection of OTC in water samples of fish farm pond, and a portable test paper was successfully fabricated for on-site visual identification of OTC. This work not only demonstrates a novel morphology of MIL-53(Al) for OTC ultrasensitive sensing but also provides an example of morphological engineering to optimize the performance of MOF-based fluorescence sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MIL-53(Al)
Open aluminum sites
Microflower
Oxytetracycline (OTC)
Fluorescent sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of OTC in water samples of fish farm pond
Preparation of paper-based sensor for visual detection of OTC
Fluorescence switch for detection of OTC
Selectivity and anti-interference capability of the probe for OTC
Detection of OTC in water samples of fish farm pond
Paper-based sensor for visual detection of OTC

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( U21A20290 , 82001957 ), the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2017YFA0207003 ), and the start found of GDUPT ( 2019rc056 , and 2019rc057 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a). Powder X-ray diffractogram patterns of FMIL-53(Al) and MIL-53(Al) along with the predicted pattern of MIL-53(Al)as and MIL-53(Al) lt based on crystallographic data (CCDC no. 220477 and 220475, respectively). b-d) SEM micrographs of FMIL-53(Al).

(a) Emission spectra of compound MIL-53(Al), FMIL-53(Al), OTC, the mixture of OTC and FMIL-53(Al), and the mixture of OTC and MIL-53(Al) in HEPES buffer. (λ ex = 400 nm). (inset) Optical images of luminescence for the mixture of OTC and FMIL-53(Al) (left) and the mixture of OTC and MIL-53(Al) (right) in HEPES buffer under 365 nm UV radiation. b) Absorption spectra of OTC (10 μM) and the system with the addition of FMIL-53(Al) suspensions (0.1 mg/mL) or MIL-53(Al) suspensions (0.1 mg/mL) in HEPES buffer.

(a) Emission spectra of FMIL-53(Al) samples upon the addition of different concentrations of OTC (0–1.5 µM) with excitation wavelength at 400 nm. b) Stern–Volmer plot of FMIL-53(Al) upon incremental addition of OTC. Error bars represent the standard deviation of F/F 0 values obtained from three independently parallel experiments. (inset) Photographs of FMIL-53(Al) suspension upon the addition of different concentrations of OTC (from left to right respectively: 0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0 and 1.5 µM) under 365 nm UV irradiation.

The selectivity of FMIL-53(Al) probe for OTC over different interfering a) antibiotics (Eryc, KNMC, SDZ, CAP, NFX, APCL, CTA, SMC, and NFZ), b) cations (K+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Na+, and Al3+), and c) anions (NO2 -, Cl-, Br-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, HPO4 2-, S2-, SO4 2-, and IO3 -). The concentrations of FMIL-53(Al), OTC, and interferents were 0.1 mg/mL, 1.5 μM, and 15 μM in the HEPES buffer, respectively. Error bars are standard deviations calculated from F/F 0 values obtained in three independent parallel experiments.

Fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F 0) of FMIL-53(Al)-OTC system after the addition of interfering a) antibiotics, b) cations, and c) anions. The concentrations of FMIL-53(Al), OTC, and the added interferents were 0.1 mg/mL, 1.5 μM, and 15 μM in the HEPES buffer. Error bars are standard deviations calculated from F/F 0 values obtained in three independent parallel experiments.

Paper-based visual detection of different concentrations of OTC: a) under natural light, b) under 365 nm ultraviolet light, the test paper loaded with FMIL-53(Al) without OTC as control; c) the fluorescence image set of the test paper upon addition of 20 μL of OTC solution at different concentrations.
(a) Structure of oxytetracycline (OTC). b) Simplified conformational equilibrium of OTC. c) Schematic illustration of the preparation of FMIL-53(Al).
Spike and recovery test of OTC in water samples of fish farm pond by the proposed sensor and conventional HPLC.

head.ce:title: Conformational fixation induced fluorescence turn-on of oxytetracycline coordinated on aluminum-based metal-organic frameworks for ultrasensitive sensing application

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: MOE Key Laboratory of Resources and Environmental System Optimization, College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the abuse of oxytetracycline (OTC) has brought serious environmental problems, it is of great practical significance to develop sensitive OTC fluorescent probes to quantify environmental OTC residues. Herein, the aluminum-based metal-organic framework MIL-53(Al) with microflower morphology and plentiful surface open aluminum sites (denoted as FMIL-53(Al)) was tailored via an acetic acid-assisted solvothermal method and subsequently exploited for ultrasensitive detection of OTC. Compared with conventional hydrothermally synthesized MIL-53(Al), FMIL-53(Al) possesses a larger specific surface with plentiful open aluminum sites, which can selectively coordinate with OTC to fix the molecular conformation. The coordinated OTC molecules undergo a transition from twisted-conformation to extended-conformation, accompanied by an increase in fluorescence quantum yield. As a fluorescent probe, the detection limit of FMIL-53(Al) for OTC is estimated as low as 0.94 nM. Finally, the FMIL-53(Al)-based probe can be applied for the detection of OTC in water samples of fish farm pond, and a portable test paper was successfully fabricated for on-site visual identification of OTC. This work not only demonstrates a novel morphology of MIL-53(Al) for OTC ultrasensitive sensing but also provides an example of morphological engineering to optimize the performance of MOF-based fluorescence sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: As a broad-spectrum antibacterial agent, oxytetracycline (OTC) ( Scheme 1a) has become one of the most commonly used drugs for the treatment of infections in animal husbandry and aquaculture due to its broad-spectrum antibacterial effect and affordability[1]. However, OTC abuse can bioaccumulate in humans through the food-chain, causing allergic reactions, gastrointestinal disorders, and liver toxicity[2,3]. In addition, the residual OTC in the environment soil or water facilitates the evolution of antibiotic-resistant bacteria, which has a devastating impact on the microecological balance[2–4]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop techniques for monitoring the residual OTC in the environment.

Although instrument-based analysis methods including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC)[5], electrochemical[6], flow injection analysis-chemiluminescence (FIA-CL)[7], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS)[8], and enzyme-linked immunoassay (ELISA)[9], can achieve highly sensitive monitoring of OTC, all these methods are time-consuming, poorly reproducible, and require sophisticated instruments as well as professional expertise. In this frame, fluorescence-based analysis methods have received extensive attention in the field of environmental pollution monitoring due to their simple operability and outstanding sensitivity, providing a new solution for the rapid, sensitive, and convenient detection of OTC[10–12]. The fluorescence sensors currently used for OTC detection are mostly “turn-off” type designed based on the energy or electron transfer process between the probe and the analyte, and rare-earth-containing sensors with a “turn-on” working mode[2,13–18]. Of these, only a handful of "turn-on" type fluorescent sensors for OTC detection have been reported, but they show higher sensitivity and selectivity which greatly reduce the false-positive rate of sensing under the same condition[19,20]. However, for the rare-earth-containing "turn-on" type sensors, notwithstanding these probes possess obvious advantages in sensitivity and selectivity, the use of rare-earth elements means higher costs, potential supply shortages, and adverse effects on the environment[21,22]. Therefore, it is worthwhile, but extremely challenging, to design a rare-earth-free "turn-on" type fluorescent probe for high sensitivity and selectivity monitoring of OTC.

Owing to their inherent merits, such as high porosity, large surface, and structural versatility, metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a type of porous hybrid material constructed by metallic nodes and organic linkers, have gained tremendous and growing research attention[23–27]. To date, an exploding number of novel MOF materials have been developed and were widely applied in fields such as separation[23], catalysis[24], chemical sensing[25], drug delivery[26], gas storage[27], and so forth. At the same time, researchers have the insight that in addition to composition and chemical structure, crystal size, morphology, and exposed facets of MOF materials can also significantly affect or even determine their properties and applications[28–35]. For MIL-53(Al), a well-known MOF material with one-dimensional diamond-shaped pores constructed from AlO4(OH)2 octahedrons and terephthalic acid ligands, different morphologies of such MOF materials, including cocoon-like[31], coin-like[32], sheet-like[33], needle-like[34], and aggregated granular-like[35], have been reported and proven to have excellent performance and potential applications in CO2 adsorption/capture[31,33], furfural separation[32], and dye adsorption[35].

Herein, for the first time, we demonstrate a facile synthesis of surface-wrinkled MIL-53(Al) microflower (denoted as FMIL-53(Al)), via an acetic acid-assisted solvothermal method (Scheme 1c). Comparatively, different from the conventional hydrothermally synthesized block-like MIL-53(Al) with a smooth surface, the wrinkled microflower morphology of FMIL-53 (Al) shows a significantly larger surface area, meanwhile, the wrinkled feature suggests poor crystallization on the crystal surface, indicating the existence of plentiful open aluminum sites on the surface of the material. These morphological merits are emphasized by its ability to significantly enhance the fluorescence of OTC molecules. Specifically, in coordination with those plentiful open aluminum sites on the surface of FMIL-53(Al), OTC molecules undergo a transition from a weakly fluorescent twisted-conformation to a strong-fluorescent extended-conformation (Scheme 1b). Furthermore, FMIL-53(Al) was used as a fluorescence sensor to explore its detection performance for OTC. Rare-earth-free FMIL-53(Al) can specifically turn on the yellow-green fluorescence of OTC centered at ~520 nm, allowing the detection of OTC as low as 0.94 nM. Finally, the FMIL-53(Al)-based probe can be applied for the detection of OTC in water samples of fish farm pond, and a portable test paper was successfully fabricated for on-site visual identification of OTC.

FMIL-53(Al) was synthesized by the acetic acid-assisted solvothermal method. Typically, 104 mg of 1, 4-dicarboxybenzene (PTA) (0.626 mmol) and 1 mL of acetic acid were dissolved in N, N’-Dimethylformamide (DMF, 10 mL) in a scintillation vial and stirred at 25 ℃ for 15 min to obtain a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, 5 mL of DMF solution of AlCl3•6 H2O (151 mg, 1.0 eq. 0.626 mmol) was transferred into the scintillation vial. The resulting mixture was then reacted at 120 °C for 72 h in a 50 mL Teflon-lined steel autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged (10, 000 rpm, 10 min) to collect the white crystals. The unreacted ligands trapped in the pore were exchanged six times in a static state with 20 mL DMF at 25 °C for 8 h each time. Next, solvent exchange was performed four times with ethanol by soaking at 25 °C for 6 h each time. Finally, the resulting white gelatinous product was vacuum dried at 85 °C for 8 h to obtain FMIL-53(Al). Yield: ca. 78% based on PTA.

MIL-53(Al) was prepared by the hydrothermal method according to a reported procedure with minor modification[36]. In brief, a 1.39 g aliquot of AlCl3•6 H2O (5.8 mmol) and 0.48 g of PTA (2.9 mmol, 0.5 eq.) in 10.0 mL of deionized water were reacted in a Teflon-lined steel autoclave under autogenous pressure at 220 ℃ for 3 days. The white crystals were obtained by centrifugation and then soaked in DMF and ethanol according to the steps in Section 2.1 . Finally, the white crystals of MIL-53(Al) were obtained by drying overnight under vacuum at 80 °C. Yield: ca. 65% based on PTA.

A stock solution of FMIL-53(Al) (2.0 mg/mL in HEPES buffer) was firstly prepared by dispersing 10.0 mg of FMIL-53(Al) into HEPES buffer (5 mL) and ultrasonic dispersion for 60 min to obtain a stable suspension. To perform titration experiments, 150 μL of the configured probe stock solution was injected into the centrifuge tube with 1.5 mL of HEPES buffer (pH = 7.3, 10 mM) solution. Then, to the probe solution, 0, 0.9, 15, 24, 30, 75, 150, 240, 300, 390, and 450 μL of 0.01 mM of OTC in HEPES buffer solution were added one by one and then bring to a final volume of 3.0 mL by adding different volumes of HEPES buffer to each centrifuge tube. Subsequently, the prepared OTC-FMIL-53(Al) systems were incubated for 1 h at 25 °C in the dark. After mixed uniformly, the spectra were measured immediately by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi) excited at 400 nm for OTC. All titrations were repeated three times under the same conditions, and the mean of the results was used to plot a curve of F/F 0 versus OTC concentration.

Stock solutions of nine interfering antibiotics (0.1 mM), nine interfering cations (0.1 mM), and nine interfering anions (0.1 mM) were prepared by dissolving solid antibiotics, cation chloride salts, and anion sodium salts in HEPES buffer. In selective experiments, FMIL-53(Al)-interferents systems were prepared by adding 150 μL of the FMIL-53(Al) stock solution, and 450 μL of 0.1 mM of interfering substances in HEPES buffer solution into 2.4 mL of HEPES buffer. For the interference study, 150 μL of the FMIL-53(Al) stock solution, 450 μL of the stock solutions of interfering substances, and 450 μL of OTC stock solution were sequentially added to centrifuge tubes containing HEPES buffer (1.95 mL). All samples were incubated for 1 h at 25 ℃ in the dark. Finally, immediately after mixing uniformly, the fluorescence spectrum of the resulting suspension was scanned.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in water samples of fish farm pond
ce:section.ce:para: The water samples were sampled from a local freshwater fish farm in Maoming, Guangdong, China, and then, sequentially filtered through 0.45 µm and 0.22 µm microporous membranes to remove possible solid suspended particles. To prepare OTC samples, added OTC solid of different masses to the obtained filtrate to give 20 μM, 40 μM, and 60 μM OTC samples. Finally, fluorescence measurements were performed as the procedure described in Section 2.3 , using OTC samples instead of OTC solutions. The HPLC conditions were as follows: analytical 4.6 × 150 mm (id) Zorbax Eclipse XDB-C8 column (5 µm particle size), mobile phase consisting of 10 mM oxalic acid-acetonitrile (80:20, v/v), flow rate: 1.0 mL/min; injection volume: 25 μL; and column temperature: 30 °C and the retention time was at 4.626 min

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of paper-based sensor for visual detection of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the FMIL-53(Al)-based fluorescence probe on the glass fiber filter paper, 20.0 mg of FMIL-53(Al) was dispersed into 10.0 mL of ethanol, and then, ultrasonic dispersion for 60 min to obtain stable suspension as the stocking FMIL-53(Al) suspension for visual detection of OTC. Then a series of 20 μL of the stocking FMIL-53(Al) suspension (2 mg/mL) was dropped onto a piece of glass fiber filter paper and air-drying under ambient conditions to form an array of OTC response paper sensors. To visualize OTC in aqueous solution, 20 μL of OTC aqueous solution with different concentrations from 5 μM to 30 μM was carefully dropped onto the center of the paper sensor and dried for 3 h in the dark at 30 ℃ under vacuum conditions. The visual detection was carried out by comparing the fluorescence changes of the test paper before and after the addition of OTC under an ultraviolet lamp with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm.

FMIL-53(Al) crystals were achieved from the carboxyl ligand (PTA) and AlCl3•6 H2O under solvothermal conditions using acetic acid as a selectivity modulator and DMF as a solvent (Scheme 1c), while MIL-53(Al) was prepared by conventional hydrothermal method at 220 °C for 72 h. As shown in Fig. 1a, the sharp diffraction peaks of MIL-53(Al) at 8.93°, 10.32°, 15.21°, 17.88°, and 26.94°, which are in agreement with simulated powder XRD of MIL-53(Al)as, suggesting successful synthesis of highly crystalline MIL-53(Al) particles. For FMIL-53(Al), it can be seen from the PXRD patterns that the product contains a mixture of phases, including MIL-53(Al)as and MIL-53(Al) lt, confirming the formation of FMIL-53(Al) crystals (Fig. 1a). However, by comparing with the XRD pattern of MIL-53(Al), the diffraction peaks of FMIL-53(Al) present relatively broadened features, indicating a somewhat poorly crystalline characteristic (Fig. 1a). In addition, the TG experimental results show a continuous weight loss (~15% weight loss) in the range of 30 to ~ 470 ℃ due to the release of the guest molecules (e.g., H2O, DMF) and indicate the high thermal stability of FMIL-53(Al) (Fig. S1 in supporting information). The main rapid weight loss between 470 °C and ~ 580 °C is attributed to the collapse of the FMIL-53(Al) framework structure and the decomposition of the PTA ligand, with the final formation of amorphous Al2O3.

The morphology of hydrothermally prepared MIL-53(Al) and acetic acid-assisted solvothermal method prepared FMIL-53(Al) are shown in Fig. S2 in supporting information and Fig. 1b-d, respectively. The MIL-53 (Al) crystals exhibit a block-like morphology with a smooth surface, confirming the high crystallinity of the hydrothermally synthesized crystals(Fig. S2 in supporting information)[37]. As shown in Figs. 1b and 1c, the FMIL-53(Al) synthesized based on the acetic acid-assisted solvothermal method exhibits a uniform and distinguishable flower-like morphology with a diameter of about 1 µm. More importantly, the surface of FMIL-53 (Al) flower-like morphology was ascertained to have obvious wrinkles, indicating poor crystallinity on the crystal surface, which implies that there exist plentiful open aluminum sites on the surface of the material (Fig. 1d). Note that this is the first report of MIL-53 (Al) with a flower-like morphology. In addition, we noticed that in the absence of acetic acid, whether at low temperatures (such as 60 °C, 80 °C, and 100 °C)[38] or high temperatures (such as 150 °C and 220 °C)[39], MIL-53(Al) crystals synthesized in pure DMF usually form nanoscale particle aggregates due to high prenucleation building unit (PNBU) supersaturation[38]. Based on those facts, we speculate that the addition of acetic acid in the reaction system can control the degree of deprotonation of the ligands, thereby reducing the nucleation rate, and ultimately leading to the growth of FMIL-53 (Al) crystals with surface-wrinkled microflower morphology[40,41].

In addition, the FT-IR and Raman spectra of MIL-53(Al), FMIL-53(Al), and free ligand (PTA) were also studied (Figs. S3 and S4 in supporting information). As shown in Fig. S3 in supporting information, two bands around 1600 cm−1 and 1410 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectra were observed, which can be identified as the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of (CO)2 in carboxylate, respectively, illustrating the Al3+ coordination of the carboxylate groups of the ligand in FMIL-53(Al) and MIL-53(Al). Besides, the peaks at 481 cm-1 are assigned as the characteristic of Al-O bands, suggesting the presence of carboxyl groups bound to Al3+ (Fig. S3 in supporting information). Additionally, the Al3+ coordination of the carboxylate in the MIL-53(Al) and FMIL-53(Al) was also proved by the observation of the symmetric stretching vibration peak of the carboxylate at 1476 cm−1 in the Raman spectra (Fig. S4 in supporting information).

As shown in Fig. 2a, FMIL-53(Al), MIL-53(Al), and OTC in the HEPES buffer did not fluoresce. Interestingly, upon addition of 3.0 μM OTC, the FMIL-53(Al)-OTC system emit yellow-green fluorescence, and under the optimal excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity centered at ~ 520 nm increased with about 180-fold increment within 30 min (Fig. 2a, Figs. S5 and S6 in supporting information). However, for MIL-53(Al), the system after the addition of 3.0 μM OTC only observed weak fluorescence light-up effect centered at 529 nm under the excitation of ultraviolet light (Fig. 2a). Additionally, MIL-53(Al) and FMIL-53(Al) in powder states exhibit almost no fluorescence, and OTC emits weak orange fluorescence. However, after detection of OTC, the used FMIL-53(Al) and MIL-53(Al) (denoted as OTC@FMIL-53(Al) and OTC@MIL-53(Al), respectively) emit dazzling green fluorescence and bright yellow-green fluorescence, respectively, under 365 nm ultraviolet light irradiation (Fig. S7 in supporting information).

To understand the origin of the fluorescence enhancement response of FMIL-53(Al) to OTC, the possible luminescence mechanism was investigated and inferred. The stability of FMIL-53(Al) during sensing tests was firstly verified by SEM, PXRD, and FT-IR spectra. As shown in Fig. S8 in supporting information, the FMIL-53(Al) after detection of OTC retained the wrinkled microflower morphology of the pristine MOF material, proving the stability of FMIL-53(Al). In addition, the PXRD pattern of FMIL-53(Al) after OTC detection showed the identical characteristic peaks to those before OTC detection, which also implies the stability of FMIL-53(Al) (Fig. S9 in supporting information). After the detection of OTC, the characteristic FT-IR peaks of the CO bond and Al-O bond of FMIL-53(Al) showed no apparent change, suggesting coordination of OTC molecules with the open aluminum sites cannot influence the coordination environment of PTA with Al3+ (Fig. S10 in supporting information). All these results illustrate the stability of FMIL-53(Al) during the sensing tests. As shown in Fig. 2b, the addition of FMIL-53(Al) resulted in an obvious red shift in the absorption peak of the absorption spectrum of OTC compared to OTC alone, accompanied by a strong fluorescence enhancement (Fig. 2a), which indicates the formation of OTC-FMIL-53(Al) binary complex[42,43]. Based on the two facts that OTC molecules exist a tendency to coordinate with metal ions [44-47] and there exist plentiful open aluminum sites distributed on the surface of the material, it is reasonable to speculate that the OTC molecules complex with the open aluminum sites on the surface of FMIL-53(Al).

In support of this hypothesis, we note that block-like MIL-53(Al) with a smooth surface or FMIL-53 (Al) coexisting with EDTA, an efficient metal ions chelating agent and presumably able to block open aluminum sites, cannot effectively enhance the fluorescence of OTC centered at ~ 520 nm (Fig. S11 in supporting information). And neither of both can cause a significant red shift of the absorption peak of OTC in the absorption spectrum (Fig. S12 in supporting information). Such results emphasize the unique merit(s) of open aluminum sites on the surface of FMIL-53(Al) in enhancing OTC fluorescence. In addition, the used FMIL-53(Al) could hardly recover its weak/non-fluorescent properties after soaking in ethanol, indicating the poor recyclability of FMIL-53(Al), which also demonstrated the strong coordination between OTC and open aluminum sites (Fig. S13 in supporting information).

After coordination with the open aluminum sites, the conformation of OTC molecules evolves from a weakly fluorescent twisted-conformation to a strong fluorescent extended-conformation (Scheme 1b)[42,43]. It has been reported that divalent metal ions (such as Mg2+ and Ca2+) can chelate with OTC molecules in a 2:1 ratio, resulting in a distinct red shift in the UV-vis spectrum and a significant enhancement in fluorescence quantum yield[44]. Typically, the formation of such complexes involves two interacting sites, the chromophore A ring and the chromophore BDC ring of OTC molecules (Scheme 1a)[44]. Such dual-site coordination drives the evolution of the conformation of OTC molecules from a twisted to an extended and rigid conformation, leading to a significant change of the lowest excited singlet states (S1) and enhancing the fluorescence of OTC[44,45]. Based on similar spectroscopic phenomena, including UV-vis spectroscopy and fluorescence emission spectroscopy, we speculate that the interaction of OTC with open aluminum sites also involves the above-mentioned dual-site coordination-driven conformational evolution, which changes the properties of the lowest excited singlet states (S1) and thus, enhances the fluorescence of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence switch for detection of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the excellent luminescence responses of FMIL-53(Al) toward OTC, we thus explored such material as a "turn-on" fluorescent probe for ultrasensitive detection of OTC. Previously, we demonstrated the time-independent and pH-independent fluorescence intensity of FMIL-53(Al) at 520 nm in deionized water. Under excitation at 400 nm, after scanning every 5 min for 2 h or every half day for 3 days, no obvious change was observed in the fluorescence emission intensity of FMIL-53(Al) at 520 nm, indicating its excellent photostability (Fig. S14 in supporting information). In addition, FMIL-53(Al) also exhibited pH-independent fluorescence stability in the pH range of 4–10 (Fig. S15 in supporting information). For the sake of determining the sensitivity of FMIL-53(Al) to OTC detection, fluorescence titration experiments were conducted. As can be seen from Fig. 3a, upon the addition of OTC, a dose-dependent increase in fluorescence centered at ~ 520 nm was recorded, and the ratio of the fluorescence intensity (F) to the initial fluorescence intensity (F 0) followed the linear equation F/F 0 = 70.9 × [OTC] (μM) + 1.03 (R 2 = 0.999) with the various concentrations of OTC (Fig. 3). Based on the generalized 3σ method, the limit of detection (LOD) of FMIL-53(Al) for OTC was determined to be as low as 0.94 nM (Fig. 3).

It is worth noting that, as a MOF-based sensor, FMIL-53(Al) exhibits ultrahigh sensitivity similar to the most sensitive MOF-based fluorescent probes in detecting OTC, such as ZIF-8/NH2-MIL-53(Al)[48], [Eu2(BIPA)3(H2O)][49], and [Tb(HL)L(H2O)]n [14] (Table S1 in supporting information). More importantly, even compared to other types of non-aptamer fluorescent probes, including sensors based on materials such as carbon dots, metal nanoclusters, coordination polymers, etc., FMIL-53(Al) can still be identified as one of the most sensitive non-aptamer fluorescent probes (Table S1 in supporting information). Incidentally, despite the slightly worse performance compared to several aptamer sensors (Table S2 in supporting information), FMIL-53(Al) possesses virtues such as convenience and economy in material preparation, making it one of the most promising OTC fluorescence sensors for practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference capability of the probe for OTC
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, to evaluate the selectivity of FMIL-53(Al) to OTC, fluorescence responses upon addition of OTC, or other interferents, including antibiotics (Eryc, KNMC, SDZ, CAP, NFX, APCL, CTA, SMC, and NFZ), cations (K+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Na+, and Al3+) and anions (NO2 -, Cl-, Br-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, HPO4 2-, S2-, SO4 2-, and IO3 -) was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4, 1.5 μM of OTC could induce 124-fold fluorescence enhancement, while all other interferents at 10-fold concentrations did not bring a significant fluorescence enhancement to the sensing system, confirming the high selectivity of FMIL-53(Al) toward OTC over other competing interferents. Meanwhile, the anti-interference capability of FMIL-53(Al) for OTC detection was further explored by competing experiments ( Fig. 5). The experimental result reveals that the coexistence of interferents of 10-fold concentration had no obvious interference on the OTC sensing, indicating the excellent anti-interference capability of FMIL-53(Al).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of OTC in water samples of fish farm pond
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the reliability of the prepared fluorescent probe in detecting OTC in actual samples, FMIL-53(Al)-based “turn-on” fluorescence probe was used in water samples of fish farm pond spiked with different amounts of OTC (0.5 μM, 1.0 μM, and 1.5 μM), and the reliability of the proposed fluorescence-based analysis method was verified by HPLC method. Table 1 shows the comparison between FMIL-53(Al)-based fluorescence sensor and conventional HPLC method for OTC detection in water samples from a fish farm pond. As shown in Table 1, the fluorescence method exhibited the recoveries ranging from 100.34% to 107.74% and the HPLC method ranging from 97.53% to 105.06% (Table 1), suggesting the consistent results between the two methods. Accordingly, the excellent analytical performance of the fluorescence method confirms the reliability of the developed FMIL-53(Al) for the detection of OTC in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Paper-based sensor for visual detection of OTC
ce:section.ce:para: Considering that the residual OTC in the environment poses a huge threat to human health and the ecological environment, there is an urgent need to develop portable analysis equipment for instant visual detection of OTC in the field. For this purpose, portable test strips were prepared as a visual detection platform, and the visual detection performance of OTC in an aqueous solution had been verified. By dropping FMIL-53(Al) ethanol suspension onto a glass fiber filter paper, followed by air drying to volatilize the solvent, the test strips were prepared and kept in the dark under vacuum conditions for future use. As a blank control group, the as-prepared test strips have almost no change under visible light ( Fig. 6a) and display dark-purple fluorescence background under a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6b). Upon addition of 20 μL of different concentrations of OTC, concentration-dependent bright yellow-green spots appeared remarkably against a dark-purple fluorescence background of the test strips under 365 nm UV irradiation, and different concentrations of OTC led to obviously distinguishable yellow-green spots with the naked eye (Fig. 6c). Thus, these strips offer a portable analytical device for quick detection of OTC in a facile way.

In summary, FMIL-53(Al) with plentiful open aluminum sites on the surface was reported for the first time, and it was applied to fluorescence sensing of OTC. Coordination with open aluminum sites promotes the evolution of OTC molecules from a weakly fluorescent twisted-conformation to a strong fluorescent extended-conformation, accompanied by an enhancement of yellow-green fluorescence centered at 520 nm. Besides, it is worth noting that, unlike most of the current fluorescence enhancement responses derived from the "antenna effect" between OTC and rare-earth cations, FMIL-53(Al) is a rare-earth-free fluorescent probe, which means low cost and sufficient supply, and environmentally friendly. In short, this work not only demonstrates a novel morphology of MIL-53(Al) for OTC ultra-sensitive sensing, but also provides an example of morphological engineering to optimize the performance of MOF-based fluorescence sensors.

Pengchen Su: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Long Yu: Methodology, Investigation. Yuejie Ai: Supervision, Methodology, Software. Songlin Zhang: Methodology, Software. Hongwei Ge: Investigation. Yiming Bu: Methodology, Investigation. Dejian Huang: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Xiangke Wang: Supervision. Suhua Wang: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (U21A20290, 82001957), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0207003), and the start found of GDUPT (2019rc056, and 2019rc057).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal ion-tetracycline interactions in biological fluids. 9. Circular dichroism spectra of calcium and magnesium complexes with tetracycline, oxytetracycline, doxycycline, and chlortetracycline and discussion of their binding
Modes Inorg. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lambs, L.; Reverend, B.D. L.; Kozlowski, H.; Berthon, G. Metal Ion-Tetracycline Interactions in Biological Fluids. 9. Circular Dichroism Spectra of Calcium and Magnesium Complexes with Tetracycline, Oxytetracycline, Doxycycline, and Chlortetracycline and Discussion of Their Binding Modes. Inorg. Chem. 1988, 27, 3001–3012. https://doi.org/10.1021/ic00290a022.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Calcium ions turn on the fluorescence of oxytetracycline for sensitive and selective detection
J. Fluor.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, H.; Peng, J.; Yu, L.; Chen, H.; Sun, M.; Sun, Z.; Ni, R.; Alamry, K.A.; Marwani, H.M.; Wang, S. Calcium Ions Turn on the Fluorescence of Oxytetracycline for Sensitive and Selective Detection. J. Fluoresc. 2020, 30, 18, 1−8, https://doi.org/10.1007/s10895–020-02512–3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of antibiotic residues in pork using paper-based microfluidic device coupled with filtration and concentration
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Nilghaz, A.; Lu, X. Detection of Antibiotic Residues in Pork Using Paper-Based Microfluidic Device Coupled with Filtration and Concentration. Anal. Chim. Acta. 2019, 1046, 163−169, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.09.041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Interface engineering of zeolite imidazolate framework-8 on twodimensional Al-metal-organic framework nanoplates enhancing performance for simultaneous capture and sensing tetracyclines
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, C.; Zhang, X.; Wen, S.; Xiang, R.; Han, Y.; Tang, W.; Yue, T.; Li, Z. Interface engineering of zeolite imidazolate framework-8 on twodimensional Al-metal-organic framework nanoplates enhancing performance for simultaneous capture and sensing tetracyclines. J. Hazard. Mater. 2020, 395, 122615−122624, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2020.122615.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Europium-based metal-organic framework containing characteristic metal chains: a novel turn-on fluorescence sensor for simultaneous high-performance detection and removal of tetracycline
Sens. Actuators, B.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, Y.; Wang, Q.; Wang, H.; Zhangsun, H.; Sun, X.; Bu, T.; Liu, Y.; Wang, W.; Xu, Z.; Wang, Li Europium-based metal-organic framework containing characteristic metal chains: A novel turn-on fluorescence sensor for simultaneous high-performance detection and removal of tetracycline. Sens. Actuators, B. 2021, 334, 129610, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.129610

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pengchen Su received his master degree in 2014 from Dalian University of Technology. He is currently a doctor’s graduate student at the College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of Metal-Organic Framework-based fluorescent probes for the detection of environmental pollutants.

Long Yu received his master degree in 2019 from North China Electric Power University. He is currently a doctor’s graduate student at the College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of fluorescent probes for the detection of environmental pollutants.

Yuejie Ai is a professor at North China Electric Power University. She received her Ph.D. degree in Theoretical Chemistry and Biology from the Royal Institute of Technology, Sweden. Her research interests focus on the computational simulations of environmental, biological and material related.

Songlin Zhang received his bachelor degree in 2019 from North China Electric Power University. He is currently a master’s graduate student at the College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University. His research interests focus on the detection and analysis of environmental pollutants using MOF materials as fluorescent probes.

Hongwei Ge received his master degree in 2017 from Sichuan Agricultural University. He is currently a doctor’s graduate student at the College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University. His research interests focus on the application of Surface-Enhanced Raman Scattering in the detection of environmental pollutants.

Yiming Bu received his bachelor degree in 2019 from Nanjing Institute of Technology. He is currently a master’s graduate student at the College of Environmental Science and Engineering, North China Electric Power University. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of fluorescent nanomaterials for environmental pollutants detection.

Dejian Huang is an associate professor at Food Science and Technology Programme, National University of Singapore. His research interests include chemical biology, particularly in design and synthesis of luminescent probes for detection of small molecules of biological importance. He has also strong interests in the chemistry of bioactive molecules for functional food development. Dejian obtained his PhD degree from Indiana University Bloomington (majoring in inorganic chemistry).

Xiangke Wang is a professor of North China Electric Power University. He received his Ph.D. degree in 2000 from Lanzhou University. Then he joined the SUBATECH Laboratory (France) as a research fellow and the Karlsruhe Research Center (Germany) as an Alexander von Humboldt research fellow. His work focuses on the synthesis of nanomaterials and their applications in energy and environmental pollution management, and also characterization for radionuclide physicochemical behavior in the environment.

Suhua Wang is a professor at the School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Guangdong University of Petrochemical Technology. He received a PhD from the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology. His current research interests focus on functional materials, analytical chemistry, and detection of hazardous substances in environment.
